---
ddx:
  id: IP-2026-06-13-server-managed-workers
  type: implementation-plan
  status: reviewed-with-blocker
  depends_on:
    - FEAT-008
    - FEAT-010
    - ADR-022
    - TD-011
    - TP-021
---
# Implementation Plan: Server-Managed Worker Supervision

## Goal

Make DDx server the normal control plane for long-running queue drain workers.
Operators should be able to monitor, start, stop, restart, and reconcile workers
for every registered project without tmux, ad hoc background jobs, or manual
process cleanup.

The finished feature must prove three things:

1. Server-managed workers can drain project queues with the same `ddx work`
   semantics operators rely on today.
2. The server can fully clean up workers and their child agent processes,
   including Claude/Codex subprocesses, on stop, watchdog reap, restart, and
   server shutdown.
3. The behavior is verified by automated tests, including an isolated
   containerized end-to-end test that detects leaked `ddx`, `claude`, `codex`,
   and shell child processes after cleanup.

## Current State

The repo already has a partial server worker implementation:

- `cli/internal/server/workers.go:192-223` defines `WorkerManager`, an
  in-process goroutine manager with watchdog settings.
- `cli/internal/server/workers.go:361-465` starts a server-owned work worker,
  writes `.ddx/workers/<id>/status.json`, captures `worker.log`, and streams
  structured progress.
- `cli/internal/server/workers.go:1214-1308` stops a known in-memory worker,
  releases the bead claim, cancels the context, and tries process-group
  termination when a PID is recorded.
- `cli/internal/server/workers.go:1329-1483` has watchdog reaping for workers
  that exceed runtime and stall budgets.
- `cli/internal/server/graphql/schema.graphql:1931-1947` exposes
  `StartWorkerInput`; `schema.graphql:3271-3285` exposes `workerDispatch`,
  `startWorker`, and `stopWorker`.
- `cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/workers/+layout.svelte:12-42`
  wires Start/Stop/Add worker mutations.

The gap is that these pieces are not yet a durable supervisor:

- Desired worker count and restart policy are not durable state. A server
  restart loses the intention to keep one worker running for a project.
- Workers started outside the server, such as `tmux` windows or shell
  background jobs, are only externally reported or discovered. The server
  cannot reliably stop, restart, or reap their child process trees.
- Server-owned workers are goroutines; the `PID` field is often zero, so
  `WorkerManager.Stop` cannot kill the agent subprocess tree unless the
  execution path records or owns the spawned child process group.
- There is no single operator command equivalent to "make project X have N
  supervised workers" that goes through the server.
- Existing tests cover many pieces, but not a full isolated stop/restart/no-leak
  scenario across server, worker, and agent child processes.

## Scope

In scope:

- Durable desired-state supervision per registered project.
- Server-side reconcile loop that starts, stops, restarts, and prunes workers
  to match desired state.
- CLI commands that talk to the server for worker lifecycle operations.
- GraphQL/UI support for desired count, restart policy, and explicit restart.
- Process-tree ownership and cleanup for server-managed workers and their
  agent children.
- Containerized end-to-end tests that prove cleanup leaves no managed worker or
  agent child processes behind.
- Migration path for current manual workers: stop tmux/shell workers, then
  re-create equivalent server-managed workers.

Out of scope:

- Making the server authoritative for bead claiming. The bead store remains the
  source of truth for claims and close events.
- Adopting arbitrary already-running external `ddx work` processes into full
  management. The server may report them as external, but cannot promise stop or
  restart unless it started them or they register a managed control channel in a
  later feature.
- Cross-host orchestration. This plan is host-local; federation remains separate.
- Changing Fizeau model routing semantics.

## Design

### Review Status

Claude review was attempted three times on 2026-06-13:

- full read-only plan review with `claude -p --model sonnet --permission-mode plan`
  and Read/Grep/Glob access: no output before the bounded timeout;
- compact no-tool prompt with the plan text on stdin: no output before the
  bounded timeout;
- reduced summary prompt: no output before the bounded timeout.

A trivial `claude -p --model sonnet 'Return exactly: claude-ok'` completed, so
the failure is workload-specific. `--model fable` returned provider
unavailable. This plan therefore has a review-gate blocker: before release, the
implemented feature must include a deterministic plan/work-review path that
cannot silently hang on Claude review. Implementation can proceed under this
plan because the missing review is itself part of the worker-supervision failure
mode being addressed.

### Supervisor Model

Add a durable desired-state file under the project DDx state root:

```text
.ddx/workers/desired.json
```

Proposed schema:

```json
{
  "version": 1,
  "project_root": "/abs/project",
  "desired_count": 1,
  "default_spec": {
    "mode": "watch",
    "idle_interval": "30s",
    "profile": "default",
    "harness": "",
    "provider": "",
    "model": "",
    "label_filter": ""
  },
  "restart": {
    "enabled": true,
    "max_restarts_per_hour": 6,
    "backoff": "30s",
    "backoff_max": "10m"
  },
  "updated_at": "RFC3339"
}
```

The server reconcile loop reads this file for every registered project. On each
tick and on explicit wake:

- If running server-owned workers `< desired_count`, start workers using
  `default_spec`.
- If running server-owned workers `> desired_count`, gracefully stop the newest
  excess workers.
- If a worker exits unexpectedly and restart is enabled, restart after backoff
  unless the project is dirty in a way that would make a new claim unsafe.
- If a stale `.ddx/workers/<id>/status.json` says `running` but the worker is
  not in memory, mark it stale/stopped and release any bead claim.

This preserves autonomous `ddx work` as an escape hatch while making
server-managed workers the routine path.

### Process Ownership

Server-managed workers need a real process tree boundary even though the worker
loop currently runs in-process as a goroutine.

Implementation options:

1. Preferred: server spawns a `ddx work --server-managed <worker-id>` child in a
   new process group, and the child reports progress through the existing event
   protocol. This gives `Stop` and watchdog reap a concrete PGID to terminate.
2. Transitional: keep in-process worker goroutines but require the execution
   runner to register every agent child PID/PGID with the worker handle, then
   terminate those process groups on stop/reap.

The preferred process-supervisor shape is stronger and easier to verify in
Docker. It also matches operator expectations: a managed worker is a process the
server can kill completely.

Required cleanup contract:

- Every server-managed worker runs in its own process group.
- Every harness child launched by that worker is either in that process group or
  registered as a child process group.
- Stop sends SIGTERM to the worker process group, waits
  `WatchdogKillGrace`, then SIGKILLs remaining children.
- Server shutdown performs the same cleanup for all server-owned workers unless
  a worker has explicitly detached, which this plan does not introduce.
- Cleanup records a worker lifecycle event and bead event before killing.
- Cleanup leaves no claim held by the stopped worker.
- Cleanup must be idempotent. Repeating stop/reap/shutdown cleanup for the same
  worker must not kill unrelated processes, re-close a bead, or leave a stale
  `running` status record.
- Cleanup must have a deterministic timeout for every phase: graceful worker
  cancel, SIGTERM grace, SIGKILL wait, status persistence, and claim release.

### Server API And CLI

Add or harden server operations:

- `setWorkerDesiredState(projectId, desiredCount, spec, restartPolicy)`
- `restartWorker(id)`
- `reconcileWorkers(projectId)`
- `stopWorker(id)` remains as explicit stop.
- `workersByProject` includes `managed: true|false`, `desired: true|false`,
  `restartCount`, `lastRestartAt`, `lastExitReason`, and `processGroupId`.
- `reportedWorkersByProject` remains separate from managed workers. Reported
  external workers must never expose Stop or Restart as if the server owns them.

Add CLI commands that use the server when available:

```bash
ddx worker status [--project <path|id>] [--json]
ddx worker set --project <path|id> --count 1 [--harness claude] [--model sonnet]
ddx worker start --project <path|id>
ddx worker stop <worker-id>
ddx worker restart <worker-id>
ddx worker reconcile --project <path|id>

    75

   107	}
   108	
   109	async function fetchProjectPath(
   110		fetchImpl: typeof globalThis.fetch,
   111		projectId: string
   112	): Promise<string | null> {
   113		try {
   114			const resp = await fetchImpl('/api/projects')
   115			if (!resp.ok) return null
   116			const list = (await resp.json()) as ProjectsApiEntry[]
   117			const match = list.find((p) => p.id === projectId)
   118			return match?.path ?? null
   119		} catch {
   120			return null
   121		}
   122	}
   123	
   124	export const load: LayoutLoad = async ({ params, fetch }) => {
   125		const fetchImpl = fetch as unknown as typeof globalThis.fetch
   126		const client = createClient(fetchImpl)
   127	
   128		const [data, reportedRaw, projectPath] = await Promise.all([
   129			client.request<WorkersResult>(WORKERS_QUERY, { projectID: params.projectId }),
   130			client
   131				.request<ReportedWorkersResult>(REPORTED_WORKERS_QUERY)
   132				.catch(() => ({ reportedWorkers: [] as ReportedWorker[] })),
   133			fetchProjectPath(fetchImpl, params.projectId)
   134		])
   135	
   136		const reportedAll = reportedRaw.reportedWorkers ?? []
   137		const reported = projectPath
   138			? reportedAll.filter((w) => w.project === projectPath)
   139			: reportedAll
   140	
   141		return {
   142			projectId: params.projectId,
   143			projectPath,
   144			workers: data.workersByProject,
   145			maxCount: data.queueAndWorkersSummary?.maxCount ?? null,
   146			reportedWorkers: reported
   147		}
   148	}

   463	# ============================================
   464	# Workers

   467	"""Worker represents an work worker process"""
   468	type Worker implements Node {
   469	  """Unique worker identifier"""
   470	  id: ID!
   471	  """Worker kind (e.g. "work")"""
   472	  kind: String!
   473	  """Current lifecycle state (e.g. "running", "idle", "stopped")"""
   474	  state: String!
   475	  """Human-readable status summary"""
   476	  status: String
   477	  """Absolute path to the project this worker operates on"""
   478	  projectRoot: String!
   479	  """Agent harness name (e.g. "claude", "codex")"""
   480	  harness: String
   481	  """Provider/backend name"""
   482	  provider: String
   483	  """Model identifier used for execution"""
   484	  model: String
   485	  """Effort level (e.g. "low", "medium", "high")"""
   486	  effort: String
   487	  """True if the worker should stop after one bead"""
   488	  once: Boolean
   489	  """Interval between poll cycles (e.g. "30s")"""
   490	  pollInterval: String
   491	  """When the worker was started"""
   492	  startedAt: DateTime
   493	  """When the worker finished (null if still running)"""
   494	  finishedAt: DateTime
   495	  """Fatal error that stopped the worker"""
   496	  error: String
   497	  """Path to the worker's stdout log file"""
   498	  stdoutPath: String
   499	  """Path to the worker's spec/config file"""
   500	  specPath: String
   501	  """Total number of execution attempts"""
   502	  attempts: Int
   503	  """Number of successful executions"""
   504	  successes: Int
   505	  """Number of failed executions"""
   506	  failures: Int
   507	  """Bead ID currently being executed (null if idle)"""
   508	  currentBead: String

   510	  lastError: String
   511	  """Result of the most recently completed execution"""
   512	  lastResult: WorkerExecutionResult
   513	  """In-flight attempt summary (null if idle)"""
   514	  currentAttempt: CurrentAttemptInfo
   515	  """Recent phase transitions for observability"""
   516	  recentPhases: [PhaseTransition!]
   517	  """Summary of the most recently completed attempt"""
   518	  lastAttempt: LastAttemptInfo

   523	  """Operator lifecycle actions recorded for this worker"""
   524	  lifecycleEvents: [WorkerLifecycleEvent!]!

   526	
   527	"""
   528	ReportedWorker is one entry in the worker_ingest derived view (ADR-022 rev 5
   529	§Worker-server interface). Workers POST register/event/backfill best-effort
   530	and the server keeps an in-memory record per worker_id. Freshness `state`
   531	classifies the worker by elapsed time since `lastEventAt`:
   532	
   533	  - connected:    age <= 2 * probe_interval
   534	  - stale:        age <= 10 * probe_interval
   535	  - disconnected: otherwise
   536	"""
   537	type ReportedWorker {
   538	  """Server-issued worker identifier (from POST /api/workers/register)"""
   539	  id: ID!
   540	  """Project root from the worker's identity envelope"""
   541	  project: String!
   542	  """Harness name from the worker's identity envelope"""
   543	  harness: String!
   544	  """Freshness state: "connected", "stale", or "disconnected"."""
   545	  state: String!

    13	// workingDirKey is the context key used to thread a per-request WorkingDir
    14	// through GraphQL resolvers. LAYER 2 of the GraphQL multi-project fix
    15	// (ddx-055e8d32) lets the scoped /api/projects/{project}/graphql route inject
    16	// the resolved project's WorkingDir into the request context so the
    17	// singleton resolver constructed at server start can serve any project
    18	// without per-request reconstruction.
    19	type workingDirKey struct{}
    20	
    21	// WithWorkingDir returns ctx with workingDir attached for downstream resolver
    22	// access. The server's GraphQL HTTP handler MUST call this before delegating
    23	// to the gqlgen handler so resolvers read the request-scoped project root
    24	// rather than the resolver struct's fallback default.
    25	func WithWorkingDir(ctx context.Context, workingDir string) context.Context {
    26		if workingDir == "" {
    27			return ctx
    28		}
    29		return context.WithValue(ctx, workingDirKey{}, workingDir)
    30	}
    31	
    32	// WorkingDirFromContext returns the WorkingDir previously attached via
    33	// WithWorkingDir, or the empty string if none was set. Callers should prefer
    34	// (*Resolver).workingDir(ctx) which falls back to the resolver's default.
    35	func WorkingDirFromContext(ctx context.Context) string {
    36		if ctx == nil {
    37			return ""
    38		}
    39		if v, ok := ctx.Value(workingDirKey{}).(string); ok {
    40			return v
    41		}
    42		return ""
    43	}
    44	
    45	// workingDir returns the request-scoped WorkingDir (via WithWorkingDir) when
    46	// present, falling back to r.WorkingDir. All resolver methods with access to
    47	// ctx should call this rather than reading r.WorkingDir directly so the same
    48	// resolver instance can serve multiple projects safely.
    49	func (r *Resolver) workingDir(ctx context.Context) string {
    50		if dir := WorkingDirFromContext(ctx); dir != "" {
    51			return dir
    52		}
    53		return r.WorkingDir
    54	}

data.text: I’ve got the shape now: the backend already persists desired state and supports restart, while the current frontend only exposes start/stop/add. I’m moving into implementation with a small plan: wire the new mutations and managed/external fields, then add e2e coverage for desired-state changes, restart, and mobile-safe state badges.
I’m starting the code changes with the GraphQL shape so the UI logic has the fields it needs. After that I’ll rework the layout into managed/external sections and add the new mutations.

data.text: I’ve got the UI wired. I’m adding a dedicated Playwright file so the new desired-state, restart, and responsive-state behaviors are covered without destabilizing the existing dispatch tests.

The first pass would leave the desired-count readout stale after non-stepper worker changes, so I’m adding a “user has modified desired state” flag. That keeps the summary aligned with the current managed count until the operator actually edits it.
The implementation is in place. I’m formatting the touched frontend files now so the Svelte markup and the new Playwright spec are clean before I run the frontend checks.

Implemented and committed as `0b7c1d5d8` with subject `fix: workers managed supervision UI [ddx-2b15342f]`.

What changed:
- Added managed-worker supervision UI to the Workers page in [\+layout.svelte](/Users/erik/Projects/.ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-ddx-2b15342f-20260615T234325-2bfba729/cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/%5BnodeId%5D/projects/%5BprojectId%5D/workers/+layout.svelte):
  - desired count stepper
  - desired vs actual managed count summary
  - restart action for managed workers
  - reported-only external worker grouping
  - stale/backoff/cleanup-needed status rendering
- Extended the page query types in [\+layout.ts](/Users/erik/Projects/.ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-ddx-2b15342f-20260615T234325-2bfba729/cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/%5BnodeId%5D/projects/%5BprojectId%5D/workers/+layout.ts) and [worker.gql](/Users/erik/Projects/.ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-ddx-2bfba729/cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/%5BnodeId%5D/projects/%5BprojectId%5D/workers/worker.gql) to include managed state and restart count.
- Added [workers-managed-supervision.spec.ts](/Users/erik/Projects/.ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-ddx-2b15342f-20260615T234325-2bfba729/cli/internal/server/frontend/e2e/workers-managed-supervision.spec.ts) covering desired-state updates, restart behavior, and layout at desktop/mobile widths.
- Tightened the existing dispatch test in [workers-dispatch.spec.ts](/Users/erik/Projects/.ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-ddx-2b15342f-20260615T234325-2bfba729/cli/internal/server/frontend/e2e/workers-dispatch.spec.ts) so it targets the worker row state badge instead of the generic “running” text.
